title: Greek Lemon Potatoes
subtitle: Oven-roasted potatoes with lemon, garlic, and oregano
excerpt: Greek lemon potatoes are cut into wedges and roasted in a generous mix of lemon juice, olive oil, stock, and dried oregano until they absorb the liquid and turn crisp at the edges. They are the standard side dish for roast lamb and grilled fish in Greek cooking.
sourceNotes: Patates lemonates, or lemon potatoes, are a staple of Greek home cooking and appear as a side dish at almost every roast meal. The method of cooking them in lemon juice and stock is characteristic of Greek vegetable dishes.

content.text: Greek lemon potatoes are wedges roasted in a stock-and-lemon mixture until the liquid is absorbed and the edges turn crisp and golden. They are the automatic side dish for Greek roast lamb and grilled meats. The method is different from standard roast potatoes: the potatoes sit in liquid rather than dry heat for the first half of cooking, which gives them a creamy interior.
Heat the oven to 200°C. Place the potato wedges in a large roasting tin in a single layer.

Mix the lemon juice, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil, {{chicken-stock}} of stock, crushed garlic, and {{oregano-dried}} of oregano. Pour over the potatoes. Season well with salt and pepper. The liquid should come about halfway up the potatoes.

Roast for 40 minutes, turning the potatoes once halfway through. Most of the liquid will be absorbed. Continue roasting for another 25 to 30 minutes until the potatoes are golden and crisp at the edges and any remaining liquid has reduced to a sticky glaze.

items.symptom: The potatoes are soft but not crisp
items.cause: Too much liquid remaining, or oven not hot enough
items.fix: Increase the oven to 220°C and roast for another 15 minutes. Make sure the tin is large enough that the potatoes are not piled on top of each other.

items.symptom: The potatoes have absorbed all the liquid and are drying out
items.cause: Too little liquid or oven too hot
items.fix: Add a splash of stock or water. The potatoes should be creamy inside even after the liquid has absorbed.

content.text: Greek lemon potatoes are the side dish that appears at almost every roast lamb meal in Greece. They can cook alongside the lamb in the same tin or in a separate tin if space allows. They are also good beside grilled fish, chicken souvlaki, or a simple green salad and feta. The lemon and herb flavour makes them distinctive.
